title: Fall Prevention in a High-Risk Hospitalized 78-Year-Old: Multifactorial Fall-Risk Assessment, the Universal-Plus-Targeted Prevention Bundle (Hourly Rounding/4 Ps, Scheduled Toileting, Bed-Exit Alarm, Med Review, Treating Orthostasis), Why Side Rails/Restraints Are NOT Fall Prevention, the POST-FALL Response, and Home-Safety Education
subtitle: FALLS are the leading cause of injury in older adults and a major preventable source of harm in the hospital (an in-hospital fall WITH INJURY is a serious, often-reportable event); consequences include HIP and other FRACTURES, HEAD INJURY (especially dangerous on ANTICOAGULANTS), loss of independence, fear of falling, and death. RISK FACTORS are INTRINSIC and EXTRINSIC. INTRINSIC: advanced age; a HISTORY OF FALLS (the single strongest predictor); gait/balance/mobility impairment and lower-extremity weakness; COGNITIVE impairment, dementia, or DELIRIUM; visual impairment; ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION; urinary urgency/frequency/nocturia or incontinence; foot problems; and chronic conditions (stroke, Parkinson disease, neuropathy). EXTRINSIC/SITUATIONAL: high-risk MEDICATIONS (benzodiazepines/sedative-hypnotics, opioids, antihypertensives/DIURETICS, hypoglycemics, anticholinergics, and POLYPHARMACY), environmental hazards (clutter, poor lighting, cords, throw rugs, wet floors), improper FOOTWEAR, the UNFAMILIAR hospital environment, tethers (IV lines, urinary catheters, telemetry), and RESTRAINTS. ASSESSMENT: screen EVERY patient with a VALIDATED fall-risk tool (e.g., the MORSE Fall Scale or Hendrich II) on admission, each shift, after a status change, and after any fall, and perform a MULTIFACTORIAL assessment (gait/balance such as the Timed Up and Go, ORTHOSTATIC vital signs, vision, medication review, cognition/delirium screen, continence, footwear, and the environment). PREVENTION uses a UNIVERSAL bundle for ALL patients PLUS TARGETED interventions for high-risk patients. UNIVERSAL: keep the CALL LIGHT and frequently used items within reach and answer calls PROMPTLY; bed in the LOW, LOCKED position; adequate LIGHTING/night-light; NON-SKID footwear; a clutter-free, dry, well-lit path; and orient the patient to the room. TARGETED (high-risk): flag the patient (wristband/sign); use a BED-/CHAIR-EXIT ALARM; perform PURPOSEFUL HOURLY ROUNDING addressing the 4 Ps (PAIN, POTTY/toileting, POSITION, POSSESSIONS); provide SCHEDULED/ASSISTED TOILETING and do NOT leave a high-risk patient alone on the toilet/commode (toileting-related falls are very common); ASSIST with ambulation and transfers using a GAIT BELT; ensure correct use of ASSISTIVE DEVICES (walker/cane) within reach; obtain a PT/OT consult; REVIEW and minimize fall-risk MEDICATIONS; treat ORTHOSTASIS (rise slowly, dangle before standing); manage delirium/pain; consider a SITTER or relocating the patient nearer the nurses' station; and use low beds/floor mats. IMPORTANTLY, SIDE RAILS and RESTRAINTS are NOT fall-prevention tools: raising ALL FOUR side rails is considered a RESTRAINT and patients climb over them (causing worse injury), and physical restraints do NOT reduce falls and increase injury, deconditioning, and death — the least-restrictive approach is used instead. POST-FALL RESPONSE: STAY WITH the patient and do NOT move them immediately; ASSESS for injury (level of consciousness, head injury — obtain neuro checks and imaging especially if anticoagulated or a head strike, pain, deformity/suspected fracture, range of motion, vital signs and orthostatics, skin); call for help; NOTIFY the provider; move the patient only after serious injury is excluded; REASSESS fall risk and UPDATE the care plan; complete an INCIDENT/OCCURRENCE REPORT (objective, factual; a quality-improvement and JUST-CULTURE tool, NOT a blame document, and NOT part of the medical record) and hold a POST-FALL HUDDLE to find the cause; and DOCUMENT objectively in the chart. EDUCATION (patient/family and discharge/HOME safety): remove throw rugs/clutter/cords, install GRAB BARS and a raised toilet seat, use non-slip mats and adequate lighting/night-lights, secure stair rails, wear proper footwear (not socks alone or loose slippers); encourage EXERCISE focused on STRENGTH and BALANCE (e.g., physical therapy, tai chi), a VISION check, a MEDICATION review, vitamin D if indicated, rising slowly to avoid orthostasis, correct assistive-device use, a personal emergency response system, and knowing how to call for help and when to seek care after a fall. NURSING ROLE: assess fall risk with a validated tool and multifactorial evaluation; implement the universal-plus-targeted prevention bundle; avoid side rails/restraints as 'fall prevention'; respond correctly to a fall (assess injury, neuro checks, notify, report, update the plan); and educate the patient and family for safety at home.

patient.name: Margaret S.
patient.code: Full code; goal = identify and reduce fall risk with a validated assessment and the prevention bundle, avoid injury, respond correctly to any fall, and prepare the patient for safe mobility at home
patient.admitDate: A 78-year-old woman is admitted to the medical unit with community-acquired pneumonia and dehydration. She has a HISTORY of a fall 3 months ago, mild cognitive impairment, urinary urgency with nocturia, and an unsteady gait for which she uses a walker at home. Her medications include furosemide and a nightly benzodiazepine (lorazepam) for sleep, and her sitting-to-standing blood pressure shows orthostatic hypotension. On a validated fall-risk screen she scores HIGH RISK. In the unfamiliar hospital environment, tethered to IV fluids, she is at substantial risk of a fall with injury. The nurse must complete a multifactorial fall-risk assessment, implement the universal-plus-targeted prevention bundle, avoid inappropriate restraints/side rails, respond correctly if she falls, and teach her and her family about home safety.
patient.room: Medical unit; high-fall-risk flagged (wristband/door sign); bed in low/locked position with bed-exit alarm; call light in reach; interdisciplinary team (nurse, PT/OT, pharmacy for medication review, provider); purposeful hourly rounding and scheduled toileting in place; discharge planning with home-safety education

vitals.temp: 37.4°C; unsteady gait (uses a walker), mild cognitive impairment, urinary urgency/nocturia, history of a recent fall, on furosemide + nightly lorazepam — scores HIGH on a validated fall-risk screen in an unfamiliar, tethered environment

37.2°C; validated tool (Morse/Hendrich II) plus multifactorial evaluation — gait/balance (Timed Up and Go), orthostatic vitals, vision, MEDICATION review (lorazepam, furosemide), cognition/delirium screen, continence, footwear, and environment

37.0°C; UNIVERSAL (call light in reach, bed LOW/locked, lighting/night-light, non-skid footwear, clutter-free path) + TARGETED (high-risk flag, BED-EXIT ALARM, purposeful HOURLY ROUNDING/4 Ps, SCHEDULED TOILETING with assistance, assisted ambulation + gait belt, walker within reach, PT/OT, medication review, treat orthostasis) — restraints/4-rails NOT used

37.0°C; despite measures she is found on the floor after trying to reach the bathroom — STAY with her, do NOT move her yet, ASSESS for injury (LOC, head strike, pain, deformity/fracture, ROM, vitals/orthostatics, skin), neuro checks (head injury), call for help, NOTIFY the provider; move only after serious injury excluded

37.0°C; no serious injury found; fall risk REASSESSED and care plan UPDATED (toileting schedule intensified, sitter considered, sedative reviewed for deprescribing); INCIDENT/OCCURRENCE report completed (factual, just-culture, not in chart); post-fall HUDDLE identifies the cause (urgent toileting + orthostasis + sedative)

37.0°C; ambulating safely with her walker; taught home safety (remove rugs/clutter, grab bars, lighting, footwear), strength/balance exercise and PT, vision/medication review, rising slowly, and a personal emergency response system; family engaged

labs.n: PRESENTATION + why falls matter + risk factors (intrinsic + extrinsic/medications)
labs.v: Margaret S., 78, admitted with pneumonia and dehydration, carries MANY fall risk factors and screens HIGH RISK. FALLS are the leading cause of injury in older adults and a major preventable hospital harm — an in-hospital fall WITH INJURY is a serious, often-reportable event — with consequences including HIP and other FRACTURES, HEAD INJURY (especially dangerous on ANTICOAGULANTS, where a ground-level fall can cause an intracranial bleed), loss of independence, fear of falling, and death. RISK FACTORS divide into INTRINSIC — a HISTORY OF FALLS (strongest predictor, present here), advanced age, gait/balance impairment and weakness (she uses a walker), COGNITIVE impairment/delirium (she has mild cognitive impairment), visual impairment, ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION (documented), urinary urgency/nocturia/incontinence (present), foot problems, and chronic neurologic disease — and EXTRINSIC/SITUATIONAL — high-risk MEDICATIONS (her benzodiazepine and diuretic; also opioids, antihypertensives, hypoglycemics, anticholinergics, polypharmacy), environmental hazards (clutter, poor lighting, cords, rugs, wet floors), improper FOOTWEAR, the UNFAMILIAR hospital environment, tethers (IV lines, catheters), and RESTRAINTS. Recognizing this constellation drives a targeted, multifactorial prevention plan.
labs.r: Falls = leading injury cause in elders + major preventable hospital harm (fall WITH injury = reportable). Consequences: hip/other fractures, HEAD bleed (esp. anticoagulated), lost independence, death. INTRINSIC risks: HISTORY OF FALLS (strongest), age, gait/balance/weakness, cognitive impairment/delirium, vision, ORTHOSTASIS, urinary urgency/nocturia. EXTRINSIC: high-risk MEDS (benzos/opioids/diuretics/antihypertensives/hypoglycemics/anticholinergics/polypharmacy), hazards, footwear, unfamiliar setting, tethers, restraints. RN role: recognize the multifactorial risk.

labs.v: Every patient is SCREENED with a VALIDATED fall-risk tool — the MORSE Fall Scale or the Hendrich II Fall Risk Model are common — on ADMISSION, each SHIFT, after any CHANGE in status (new sedative, post-op, new delirium), and after ANY fall. A high score triggers the high-risk prevention bundle. Beyond the score, the nurse performs a MULTIFACTORIAL assessment to target modifiable causes: GAIT and BALANCE (e.g., the Timed Up and Go test, ability to transfer/ambulate, use of an assistive device); ORTHOSTATIC vital signs (lying/sitting/standing — a drop with symptoms is a key reversible cause); VISION; a MEDICATION review for fall-risk drugs (sedatives/benzodiazepines, opioids, diuretics/antihypertensives, hypoglycemics, anticholinergics, polypharmacy — candidates for timing changes or deprescribing); COGNITION and a DELIRIUM screen (CAM); CONTINENCE and toileting needs (urgency/nocturia greatly raises risk); FOOTWEAR (non-skid vs socks/loose slippers); and the ENVIRONMENT (clutter, lighting, cords, bed height, tethers, call-light reach). For Margaret, the assessment flags her history of falls, orthostasis, the benzodiazepine and diuretic, urinary urgency, walker dependence, and the unfamiliar tethered environment as the targets for intervention.
labs.r: Screen with a VALIDATED tool (MORSE / Hendrich II) on admission, each shift, after status change, and after any fall. Add a MULTIFACTORIAL assessment: gait/balance (Timed Up and Go), ORTHOSTATIC vitals, vision, MEDICATION review (sedatives/diuretics/etc.), cognition/delirium (CAM), continence/toileting, footwear, environment. Target the modifiable causes. RN role: validated screen + multifactorial evaluation.

labs.n: PREVENTION BUNDLE — universal + targeted; why side rails/restraints are NOT fall prevention
labs.v: Prevention combines a UNIVERSAL bundle for ALL patients with TARGETED measures for high-risk patients. UNIVERSAL: keep the CALL LIGHT and needed items within REACH and answer calls PROMPTLY; bed in the LOW and LOCKED position; adequate LIGHTING/night-light; NON-SKID footwear; a dry, CLUTTER-FREE, well-lit path; and orient the patient to the room/routine. TARGETED for high risk: visibly FLAG the patient (wristband/door sign); use a BED-/CHAIR-EXIT ALARM; perform PURPOSEFUL HOURLY ROUNDING covering the 4 Ps — PAIN, POTTY (offer toileting), POSITION (comfort/repositioning), and POSSESSIONS (within reach); provide SCHEDULED, ASSISTED TOILETING and do NOT leave a high-risk patient alone on the toilet/commode (toileting falls are very common); ASSIST with ambulation/transfers using a GAIT BELT and ensure the ASSISTIVE DEVICE (walker/cane) is correct and within reach; obtain a PT/OT consult; REVIEW and minimize fall-risk MEDICATIONS (deprescribe the benzodiazepine, time the diuretic earlier); TREAT ORTHOSTASIS (rise slowly, dangle before standing, hydrate); manage delirium/pain; consider a SITTER or moving the patient nearer the nurses' station; and use LOW beds/floor mats. CRITICAL TEACHING POINT: SIDE RAILS and RESTRAINTS are NOT fall-prevention tools — raising ALL FOUR side rails is legally a RESTRAINT and patients try to CLIMB OVER them (causing worse injury), and physical restraints do NOT prevent falls while increasing injury, deconditioning, agitation, and death; the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE approach (the bundle above) is used instead. Half/two side rails as a mobility aid to assist repositioning are acceptable, but rails are never the fall-prevention strategy.
labs.r: UNIVERSAL bundle (all): call light + items in REACH + answer promptly, bed LOW/locked, lighting/night-light, NON-SKID footwear, clutter-free path, orient. TARGETED (high risk): flag, BED-EXIT ALARM, purposeful HOURLY ROUNDING (4 Ps: Pain/Potty/Position/Possessions), SCHEDULED assisted TOILETING (don't leave alone), assisted ambulation + gait belt, assistive device in reach, PT/OT, MED review/deprescribe, treat orthostasis, sitter/near station, low bed/mats. SIDE RAILS (4 up) + RESTRAINTS are NOT fall prevention (= restraint, climb-over injury, more harm) — least-restrictive instead. RN role: universal + targeted bundle, no restraints.

labs.n: POST-FALL response + incident reporting / just culture
labs.v: If a fall occurs, the response is structured: STAY WITH the patient and do NOT move them immediately; ASSESS for injury — level of consciousness, HEAD injury (obtain NEURO CHECKS and consider CT, ESPECIALLY if the patient is on an ANTICOAGULANT or struck the head), PAIN, deformity/suspected FRACTURE, range of motion, VITAL SIGNS and orthostatics, and skin/lacerations; CALL FOR HELP and do not lift alone; NOTIFY the provider; and move the patient only AFTER serious injury is excluded (using safe technique/lift equipment). Then REASSESS fall risk and UPDATE the care plan to address the cause (here: urgent toileting plus orthostasis plus a sedative). Complete an INCIDENT/OCCURRENCE REPORT — an OBJECTIVE, FACTUAL account used for QUALITY IMPROVEMENT and a JUST-CULTURE review (focused on system causes, NOT blaming the patient or staff); the incident report is NOT filed in the medical record and is NOT referenced in the chart (chart documentation is a separate, objective note of the assessment and care). A POST-FALL HUDDLE with the team identifies contributing factors and immediate fixes. Continuous monitoring follows, especially for delayed injury (e.g., a slow intracranial bleed in an anticoagulated patient — serial neuro checks). For Margaret, no serious injury is found, the plan is intensified (toileting schedule, sitter consideration, sedative deprescribing), and the event is reported and analyzed.
labs.r: POST-FALL: STAY with patient + do NOT move immediately; ASSESS injury (LOC, HEAD/neuro checks + CT esp. if anticoagulated/head strike, pain, deformity/fracture, ROM, vitals/orthostatics, skin); call for help; NOTIFY provider; move only after serious injury excluded. REASSESS risk + UPDATE plan. INCIDENT/OCCURRENCE report = objective, just-culture/QI tool, NOT in the chart, NOT blame; post-fall HUDDLE for cause; document objectively in chart; watch for delayed injury (serial neuro checks). RN role: assess + protect + notify + report + update plan.

labs.n: Education / home safety + nursing role; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: EDUCATION (patient/family + discharge/HOME safety) reduces recurrence: modify the HOME — remove THROW RUGS, clutter, and cords; install GRAB BARS and a raised toilet seat in the bathroom; use NON-SLIP mats and adequate LIGHTING/night-lights; secure STAIR RAILS; store items at reachable height; and wear proper FOOTWEAR (not socks alone or loose slippers). Encourage EXERCISE focused on STRENGTH and BALANCE (physical therapy, tai chi), a VISION check, a MEDICATION review to reduce fall-risk drugs, VITAMIN D if indicated, rising SLOWLY to avoid orthostasis, correct ASSISTIVE-DEVICE use, a PERSONAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE SYSTEM, and knowing how to call for help and when to seek care after a fall (head injury, inability to bear weight). PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT rely on raising all four side rails or on physical restraints to 'prevent' falls; did NOT leave the high-risk patient alone on the toilet/commode; did NOT ignore orthostasis or the sedating medications; did NOT move her immediately after the fall before assessing for injury; and did NOT treat the incident report as a punitive/blame document or place it in the chart. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: validated screening with multifactorial assessment; the universal-plus-targeted bundle (hourly rounding/4 Ps, scheduled toileting, bed-exit alarm, assisted ambulation, medication review, orthostasis treatment); least-restrictive care without restraints; a correct post-fall response with reassessment and just-culture analysis; and thorough home-safety education with PT/OT and family engagement. EXPECTED outcome: fall risk is reduced, injury is prevented or promptly managed, the cause is addressed, and the patient is prepared for safe mobility at home. NURSING ROLE: assess fall risk (validated tool + multifactorial); implement the universal-plus-targeted prevention bundle; avoid side rails/restraints as fall prevention; respond correctly to a fall and report/analyze it; and educate the patient and family.
labs.r: EDUCATE/home safety: remove rugs/clutter/cords, GRAB BARS + raised toilet seat, non-slip mats, lighting/night-lights, stair rails, proper FOOTWEAR; strength/BALANCE exercise (PT/tai chi), vision check, MEDICATION review, vitamin D, rise slowly, assistive-device use, PERS, when to seek care. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no 4-side-rails/restraints as prevention, didn't leave alone on toilet, didn't ignore orthostasis/sedatives, didn't move before injury assessment, didn't treat incident report as blame/put in chart. PROTECTIVE: validated screen + bundle + least-restrictive + correct post-fall + home teaching/PT/family. RN role: assess + prevent + respond + educate.

nursesNote: Medical unit — Margaret S., 78, admitted with pneumonia + dehydration; HIGH FALL RISK. Risk factors: HISTORY of a fall 3 mo ago (strongest predictor), mild cognitive impairment, unsteady gait/walker, urinary URGENCY + nocturia, ORTHOSTATIC hypotension (138/82 sit → 116/70 stand), meds = FUROSEMIDE + nightly LORAZEPAM (benzodiazepine), unfamiliar tethered (IV) environment. ASSESSMENT: validated tool (MORSE/Hendrich II) on admission/each shift/status change/after any fall + MULTIFACTORIAL eval — gait/balance (TUG), orthostatic vitals, vision, MEDICATION review, cognition/delirium (CAM), continence/toileting, footwear, environment. PREVENTION = UNIVERSAL (call light + items in REACH + answer promptly, bed LOW/locked, lighting/night-light, NON-SKID footwear, clutter-free path, orient) + TARGETED (flag high-risk, BED-EXIT ALARM, purposeful HOURLY ROUNDING/4 Ps = Pain/Potty/Position/Possessions, SCHEDULED assisted TOILETING — don't leave alone on toilet/commode, assisted ambulation + GAIT BELT, walker in reach, PT/OT, MED review/deprescribe benzo + time diuretic, TREAT ORTHOSTASIS [rise slowly/dangle/hydrate], sitter/near nurses' station, low bed/mats). SIDE RAILS (all 4 up = RESTRAINT, climb-over injury) + RESTRAINTS are NOT fall prevention (more harm; least-restrictive instead — links restraint case 110). POST-FALL (found on floor reaching for bathroom): STAY with her + do NOT move immediately; ASSESS injury (LOC, HEAD/neuro checks ± CT esp. if anticoagulated/head strike, pain, deformity/fracture, ROM, vitals/orthostatics, skin); call for help; NOTIFY provider; move only after serious injury excluded. REASSESS risk + UPDATE plan (intensify toileting, consider sitter, review sedative). INCIDENT/OCCURRENCE report = objective/factual, JUST-CULTURE + QI tool, NOT in chart, NOT blame; post-fall HUDDLE for cause (urgent toileting + orthostasis + sedative); document objectively; watch delayed injury (serial neuro checks). EDUCATE/home safety: remove rugs/clutter/cords, GRAB BARS + raised toilet seat, non-slip mats, lighting/night-lights, stair rails, proper FOOTWEAR; strength/BALANCE exercise (PT/tai chi), vision check, med review, vitamin D, rise slowly, assistive-device use, PERS. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no 4-rails/restraints as prevention, didn't leave alone on toilet, didn't ignore orthostasis/sedatives, didn't move before injury assessment, didn't treat report as blame/put in chart. Outcome: no serious injury; risk reduced; safe ambulation with walker; family engaged.
providerOrders: ADMIT: medical unit; community-acquired pneumonia + dehydration; HIGH fall risk

FALL RISK: screen with a validated tool (Morse/Hendrich II) on admission, each shift, after status change, and after any fall; multifactorial assessment (gait/balance, orthostatic vitals, vision, medications, cognition/delirium, continence, footwear, environment)
Call light and needed items within reach; answer calls promptly; bed low and locked; adequate lighting/night-light; non-skid footwear; clutter-free, dry, well-lit path; orient to room
Flag high risk (wristband/sign); bed-/chair-exit alarm; purposeful hourly rounding (4 Ps: pain, potty/toileting, position, possessions)
Scheduled, assisted toileting; do NOT leave the patient alone on the toilet/commode; assist ambulation/transfers with a gait belt; assistive device (walker) within reach

PT/OT consult; pharmacy medication review to minimize fall-risk drugs (deprescribe benzodiazepine; adjust diuretic timing); treat orthostasis (rise slowly, dangle, hydrate); manage delirium/pain; consider sitter or relocation near the nurses' station; low bed/floor mats
Do NOT use four side rails up or physical restraints as fall prevention (least-restrictive approach)

Stay with the patient; do not move until assessed; evaluate for injury (LOC, head/neuro checks and imaging if head strike or anticoagulated, pain, fracture, ROM, vitals/orthostatics, skin); notify provider; move only after serious injury excluded

Reassess fall risk and update the plan; complete an incident/occurrence report (objective, just-culture, not in the chart); post-fall huddle; document objectively; serial neuro checks if head injury suspected

EDUCATION/DISCHARGE: home-safety modifications (remove rugs/clutter/cords, grab bars, raised toilet seat, lighting, stair rails, footwear), strength/balance exercise (PT/tai chi), vision and medication review, vitamin D if indicated, assistive-device use, personal emergency response system, and how/when to seek help after a fall

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this high-fall-risk patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A history of a recent fall plus unsteady gait/walker use, mild cognitive impairment, and urinary urgency/nocturia — strong intrinsic fall risk factors
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these intrinsic factors (especially a prior fall) make her high-risk. RN role: recognize intrinsic risk.

opts.text: Orthostatic hypotension and fall-risk medications (a benzodiazepine and a diuretic) that can be modified
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — orthostasis and sedating/diuretic drugs are modifiable, high-yield contributors. RN role: identify modifiable causes.

opts.text: Screen with a validated fall-risk tool and perform a multifactorial assessment (gait/balance, orthostatic vitals, vision, meds, cognition, continence, footwear, environment)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — validated screening plus multifactorial assessment targets the plan. RN role: assess fall risk.

opts.text: Implement the universal-plus-targeted prevention bundle (call light in reach, low bed, bed-exit alarm, hourly rounding/4 Ps, scheduled toileting, assisted ambulation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bundle is the core of prevention. RN role: implement universal + targeted measures.

opts.text: The unfamiliar, tethered hospital environment (IV lines) and improper footwear add extrinsic/situational risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — situational/environmental factors are modifiable and important. RN role: address the environment.

opts.text: Know the correct post-fall response and that side rails/restraints are NOT fall prevention
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — restraints/4-rails cause harm, and a correct post-fall response is essential. RN role: least-restrictive care + correct post-fall response.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — documented for the record
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Raise all four side rails and apply a vest restraint to keep her in bed and prevent falls
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Four side rails up is a restraint (patients climb over → worse injury), and restraints do not prevent falls and increase harm; use the least-restrictive bundle.

opts.text: Leave the high-risk patient alone on the toilet to give her privacy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Toileting-related falls are very common; a high-risk patient should be assisted and not left alone on the toilet/commode.

opts.text: A history of falls is the single strongest predictor of future falls, and risk is both intrinsic and extrinsic/situational
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk-factor framework. Foundation for assessment.

opts.text: Fall risk is screened with a validated tool on admission, each shift, after a status change, and after any fall, with a multifactorial assessment to target causes
opts.rat: CORRECT — assessment principle. Foundation for a targeted plan.

opts.text: Prevention combines universal precautions for all with targeted interventions for high-risk patients (alarms, hourly rounding, scheduled toileting, assisted ambulation, medication review)
opts.rat: CORRECT — universal-plus-targeted bundle. Foundation for prevention.

opts.text: Orthostatic hypotension and sedating/diuretic medications are modifiable contributors that should be treated or reviewed
opts.rat: CORRECT — modifiable-cause principle. Foundation for targeted intervention.

opts.text: Side rails (all four up) and physical restraints are not fall-prevention tools and can increase injury; the least-restrictive approach is used
opts.rat: CORRECT — the restraint/side-rail principle. Foundation for safe, least-restrictive care.

opts.text: After a fall, the patient is assessed for injury before being moved, the provider is notified, and an objective incident report is completed for quality improvement
opts.rat: CORRECT — post-fall response principle. Foundation for correct handling.

opts.text: Applying physical restraints is the most effective evidence-based way to prevent falls
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraints do NOT prevent falls and increase injury, deconditioning, and death; least-restrictive interventions are used.

opts.text: An incident report should be placed in the medical record and used to discipline the staff involved
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The incident report is a separate quality-improvement/just-culture document, not part of the chart, and is not a blame/discipline tool.

opts.text: Screen fall risk with a validated tool and complete a multifactorial assessment to identify modifiable causes
opts.rat: #1 — Assessment identifies who is at risk and why, driving the whole plan. RN role: validated screen + multifactorial assessment.

opts.text: Implement the universal-plus-targeted prevention bundle (call light, low bed, alarm, hourly rounding, scheduled toileting, assisted ambulation)
opts.rat: #2 — The bundle is the core preventive intervention once risk is identified. RN role: implement the bundle.

opts.text: Treat modifiable contributors — orthostasis, fall-risk medications, toileting needs — and consult PT/OT
opts.rat: #3 — Addressing the specific causes reduces risk further. RN role: treat modifiable factors.

opts.text: Respond correctly to any fall (assess for injury before moving, neuro checks, notify, report) and avoid restraints/side rails as prevention
opts.rat: #4 — Correct post-fall response and least-restrictive care prevent additional harm. RN role: safe response + least-restrictive care.

opts.text: Educate the patient and family on home safety and arrange follow-up/PT for safe mobility after discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Education and follow-up sustain prevention beyond the hospital. RN role: home-safety teaching.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this high-fall-risk patient.

opts.text: Keep the call light and needed items within reach, the bed low and locked, the path clear, and provide non-skid footwear
opts.rat: Universal fall precautions for all patients. RN role: universal bundle.

opts.text: Use a bed-exit alarm, perform purposeful hourly rounding (4 Ps), and provide scheduled, assisted toileting
opts.rat: Targeted high-risk interventions that prevent unassisted attempts. RN role: targeted bundle.

opts.text: Review and minimize fall-risk medications (deprescribe the benzodiazepine, adjust diuretic timing) and treat orthostasis (rise slowly, hydrate)
opts.rat: Addresses modifiable pharmacologic and physiologic causes. RN role: treat modifiable factors.

opts.text: Assist ambulation/transfers with a gait belt, ensure correct walker use, and obtain a PT/OT consult
opts.rat: Mobility support reduces falls while preserving function. RN role: assisted mobility + PT/OT.

opts.text: Provide home-safety education (remove rugs, grab bars, lighting, footwear) and strength/balance exercise referral for discharge
opts.rat: Education and rehabilitation sustain prevention after discharge. RN role: home-safety teaching.

opts.text: If a fall occurs, stay with the patient, assess for injury before moving, perform neuro checks if indicated, and notify the provider
opts.rat: Correct post-fall response that prevents additional harm. RN role: safe post-fall response.

opts.text: Raise all four side rails and apply a vest restraint to keep the patient in bed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — four rails up is a restraint (climb-over injury) and restraints do not prevent falls and increase harm; use least-restrictive measures.

opts.text: Leave the high-risk patient alone on the commode to finish toileting
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — toileting falls are common; stay with/assist the high-risk patient rather than leaving them alone.

opts.text: After a fall, immediately lift the patient back to bed before assessing for injury
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — assess for injury (including head/spine/fracture) before moving; moving first can worsen an injury.

opts.text: File the incident report in the patient's chart and use it to identify who is to blame
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the incident report is a separate just-culture/QI document, not part of the chart, and is not used to assign blame.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Screen fall risk with a validated tool and complete a multifactorial assessment
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Identifying risk and its causes drives the plan. RN role: assess fall risk.

opts.text: Implement universal precautions plus targeted high-risk measures (alarm, hourly rounding, scheduled toileting, assisted ambulation, call light in reach)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The bundle puts prevention into action. RN role: implement the bundle.

opts.text: Treat modifiable contributors — review/adjust fall-risk medications, treat orthostasis, consult PT/OT
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Targeting specific causes further reduces risk. RN role: treat modifiable factors.

opts.text: If a fall occurs, stay with the patient, assess for injury before moving, perform neuro checks if indicated, notify the provider, and complete an incident report/huddle
opts.rat: #4 — When a fall happens. A correct, structured response limits harm and improves the system. RN role: post-fall response + report.

opts.text: Reassess and update the plan and provide home-safety education for safe mobility at discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/discharge. Reassessment and education sustain prevention. RN role: update plan + educate.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse screened fall risk with a validated tool, kept the bed low with the call light in reach, and used a bed-exit alarm with purposeful hourly rounding
opts.rat: Validated screening plus the universal/targeted bundle is correct fall-prevention practice. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse provided scheduled, assisted toileting and did not leave the high-risk patient alone on the commode
opts.rat: Toileting falls are common; assisted, scheduled toileting is correct. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse reviewed fall-risk medications for deprescribing, treated orthostasis, and consulted PT/OT
opts.rat: Addressing modifiable causes reduces risk. Correct practice.

opts.text: After the fall, the nurse stayed with the patient, assessed for injury and performed neuro checks before moving her, and notified the provider
opts.rat: A correct, structured post-fall response prevents additional harm. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse completed an objective incident report for quality improvement and held a post-fall huddle to find the cause
opts.rat: Just-culture, QI-focused reporting and analysis improve safety. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse raised all four side rails and applied a vest restraint to prevent the patient from getting up
opts.rat: ⚠️ Four rails up is a restraint (climb-over injury) and restraints do not prevent falls and increase harm. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse left the high-risk patient alone on the toilet to answer another call light
opts.rat: ⚠️ Leaving a high-risk patient alone on the toilet risks a common, preventable fall. Improper practice.

opts.text: After the fall, the nurse immediately lifted the patient back into bed before assessing for injury
opts.rat: ⚠️ Moving before assessing for head/spine/fracture injury can worsen harm. Improper practice.

opts.text: The nurse documented the incident report in the medical record and named the staff at fault
opts.rat: ⚠️ The incident report is a separate just-culture/QI document, not part of the chart, and is not a blame tool. Improper practice.

opts.text: Before discharge, the nurse taught the patient and family about removing rugs, installing grab bars, proper footwear, and balance exercise
opts.rat: Home-safety education sustains prevention after discharge. Correct practice.
